countries: Hawaii (United States — Midway, Kure, Pearl and Hermes Atolls)
nativeRange.habitat: Adults inhabit rocky and coral reefs in subtropical to temperate waters, typically encountered as pairs ranging over rocky substrate with mixed coral and macroalgae growth

diet.notes: In the wild, the Japanese Pygmy Angelfish has been documented feeding on algae, benthic invertebrates (small animals living on the reef surface), and sponges. In captivity, a varied diet is generally advised: Spirulina, dried marine algae sheets, high-quality prepared angelfish foods, and meaty items such as vitamin-enriched brine shrimp and mysis shrimp. Offering small amounts 2–3 times daily tends to reduce coral-nipping behavior, as a consistently well-fed fish is less likely to investigate corals for food. Live rock with established algae growth and microfauna also helps satisfy the species' natural grazing instincts.

avoidWith: very small, passive fish that may be intimidated
LPS-dominated tanks where nipping is unacceptable

summary: The Japanese Pygmy Angelfish (Centropyge interrupta) is a rare, collector-grade dwarf angelfish from rocky reefs off Japan and the far northwestern Hawaiian Islands, recognized by its deep-blue body and vivid yellow saddle. It requires cool water and specialist-level care.
careNotes: The Japanese Pygmy Angelfish has historically been wild-collected from deep reefs in Japan and the northwestern Hawaiian Islands, and is now also produced in limited numbers by specialist captive-breeding programs. Wild-caught specimens command very high prices — commonly USD 2,500–3,500 — and are best suited to advanced keepers with experience in demanding reef fish. Temperature is a key husbandry consideration: this species ranges into temperate Japanese waters and is collected from depths of 12–60 m, so wild-caught individuals tend to do best at the cooler end of the reef-aquarium range (around 20–24°C). Captive-bred juveniles have been reported to adapt well to standard tropical reef temperatures up to around 28°C. Wild specimens collected from depths greater than around 30 m may have experienced decompression stress during collection and benefit from extended quarantine and gradual, dimly lit acclimation. Reef compatibility is conditional: the species is generally considered safe with SPS corals (small-polyp stony corals), but has been reported to nip LPS polyps (large-polyp stony corals), zoanthids, Montipora, and clam mantles, particularly when underfed. The Japanese Pygmy Angelfish should be kept singly unless acquired as an established pair, and should not be combined with other dwarf angelfish (Centropyge) species. The aquarium should provide plenty of rockwork with caves and overhangs alongside open swimming space; algae growth on live rock supports the fish's natural grazing behavior.
breedingNotes: The Japanese Pygmy Angelfish is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals begin life as females and the dominant fish in a pair transitions to male. In the wild, pairs form lasting monogamous bonds rather than the shifting harems seen in many other dwarf angelfish. The species spawns by releasing eggs and sperm into open water, where fertilized eggs develop as part of the plankton before settling. Captive breeding has been achieved by specialist researchers and by ORA, but the larviculture (the rearing of newly hatched, microscopic larvae) required is highly demanding and well outside the scope of typical home aquariums. ORA-bred juveniles enter the trade periodically and are generally considered hardier and more adaptable to a range of temperatures than their wild-caught counterparts.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Primary source after LiveAquaria product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/japanese-pygmy-angelfish) returned 404 and Bluezoo Aquatics page also 404'd. FishBase supplied taxonomy, depth range (12–60 m), max TL (15 cm), monandric/protogynous sex change with length-at-change (12.7 cm TL) and transition time (20–39 days), monogamous pair behavior, native range, and IUCN status (LC, 2009).

additional.name: Fishkeeper UK
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (15 cm), temperature (23–26°C tropical), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (SG 1.020–1.025), reef compatibility ('with caution' — may nip LPS, zoanthids, clam mantles), and diet/feeding frequency (small amounts 3× per day). Documents small-scale commercial captive breeding.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Centropyge interrupta
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name and authority, family Pomacanthidae, native range (Japan + Midway/Kure/Pearl and Hermes), depth (15–60 m), max length (15 cm TL, sex change at 12.7 cm), monogamous pairing, and successful captive breeding. Diet noted as algae, benthic invertebrates, and sponges.

additional.name: Marine Collectors — Interruptus Angelfish
additional.notes: Used for temperature guidance (collector-source recommendation 18–23°C / 65–73°F for wild specimens, vs Fishkeeper's 23–26°C — averaged to 20–25°C in waterParameters; spread documented in careNotes), reef-safety nuance (concern primarily for Montipora vs Acropora-safe), price tier ($2,500–3,500), and bold-personality temperament notes.

additional.name: Reefs.com — Japanese Pygmy Angelfish article (via web search)
additional.notes: Web-search excerpt provided tank-size guidance (30–55 gal juvenile, 75 gal adult — driving 300 L floor) and confirmation that captive-bred juveniles adapt to warmer tropical temperatures up to ~28°C. Full page fetch was 403-blocked; reefs.com/fish/interruptus/ also 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite (all individuals start as female; the dominant fish in a bonded pair transitions to male) that forms monogamous pairs rather than harems — atypical for dwarf angelfish. Spawns by releasing eggs and sperm into open water. Captive rearing has been achieved by ORA and specialist researchers but requires dedicated larviculture facilities beyond the scope of typical home aquariums.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Wild-caught specimens commonly sell for USD 2,500–3,500, require cooler water than most reef aquariums, and may carry decompression stress from deep collection. Captive-bred individuals are more forgiving but still uncommon. This species is best suited to experienced reef keepers prepared for its specialized requirements.
